question_text: A wheelbarrow has its wheel (pivot) 0.4 m from the load (a pile of soil). The handles are 1.6 m from the pivot. The soil weighs 200 N. A gardener lifts the handles with an upward effort force. (a) Calculate the effort force needed to just lift the wheelbarrow when balanced. (b) Explain why the wheelbarrow is considered a useful lever, and describe what happens to the stability of the wheelbarrow if the centre of gravity of the soil is moved further forward (closer to the wheel).
correct_answer: (a) Effort force = (200 × 0.4) ÷ 1.6 = 80 ÷ 1.6 = 50 N. (b) The wheelbarrow is useful because the effort distance (1.6 m) is 4 times larger than the load distance (0.4 m), so a small effort force (50 N) can lift a much larger load (200 N). If the centre of gravity moves closer to the wheel, it moves closer to the pivot, which decreases the moment needed to balance it, making it easier to lift but less stable because the load is more directly above the pivot point.
explanation: Part (a) tests calculation using the principle of moments. Part (b) requires students to explain why a wheelbarrow is an effective lever (mechanical advantage through distance ratio) and to understand how centre of gravity affects both the moment calculation and the stability of the system. Moving the centre of gravity closer to the pivot reduces the turning effect but makes the wheelbarrow more likely to tip if tilted sideways, showing deeper understanding of moments and stability.
